C:
For a typical mobile app, default to **REST**, and switch to **GraphQL** only when you have a concrete driver — most commonly: many screens with very different and frequently-changing data needs, and one team owning both the app and the API.

Here's the reasoning, weighted toward what actually bites you on mobile specifically.

**Why REST is the safer default**

- **HTTP caching is free and mature.** REST resources cache cleanly at the CDN, proxy, and client layers via standard cache headers. That directly cuts latency and data usage on flaky mobile networks. GraphQL usually POSTs every request to a single endpoint, so you lose HTTP-layer caching and have to rebuild it client-side with a normalized cache (Apollo/Relay/urorg).
- **Operationally simpler and easier to secure.** No resolver design, no N+1 query traps, and no need for query depth/cost limiting to stop a malicious or buggy client from issuing an expensive nested query. The attack surface is smaller and more familiar.
- **Easier to staff and reason about.** More engineers know REST cold; debugging is just looking at a URL and a status code.

**Why mobile, specifically, pushes some teams to GraphQL**

These are the two mobile pains GraphQL genuinely solves better than REST:

- **Over- and under-fetching.** Mobile screens each want a slightly different slice of data, and bandwidth matters. GraphQL lets each screen request exactly the fields it needs in one round trip, instead of either bloated payloads or a waterfall of REST calls. On a constrained connection this is a real win.
- **Version-locked clients.** Users run old app versions for months — you can't force-upgrade them. GraphQL's additive schema evolution (deprecate fields, never break) handles this more gracefully than spinning up `/v2`, `/v3` REST endpoints you then maintain forever. (Note this is exactly the problem GraphQL was built at Facebook to solve — for their mobile app.)

**What should decide it for you**

- Choose **GraphQL** if: you control both client and server, you have many varied screens with volatile data requirements, you're iterating product fast, and the team can absorb the extra server-side discipline (caching, query-cost limits, schema governance).
- Choose **REST** if: the API is mostly straightforward CRUD, you lean on CDN/HTTP caching, you have third-party or external consumers, or the team has no GraphQL experience and you want fewer moving parts.

A practical middle path worth knowing: you can get most of GraphQL's mobile benefit by putting a **backend-for-frontend (BFF)** in front of REST services — purpose-built compound endpoints shaped for each screen. That captures "one round trip, exactly the right data" without taking on a full GraphQL runtime.

If you can tell me how many distinct screens/data shapes you expect, whether one team owns both ends, and how much you rely on caching, I can turn this into a firm single pick rather than a conditional one.

Here's the reasoning, weighted toward what actually bites you on mobile specifically.

**Why REST is the safer default**

- **HTTP caching is free and mature.** REST resources cache cleanly at the CDN, proxy, and client layers via standard cache headers. That directly cuts latency and data usage on flaky mobile networks. GraphQL usually POSTs every request to a single endpoint, so you lose HTTP-layer caching and have to rebuild it client-side with a normalized cache (Apollo/Relay/urorg).

- **Easier to staff and reason about.** More engineers know REST cold; debugging is just looking at a URL and a status code.

**Why mobile, specifically, pushes some teams to GraphQL**

These are the two mobile pains GraphQL genuinely solves better than REST:

- **Over- and under-fetching.** Mobile screens each want a slightly different slice of data, and bandwidth matters. GraphQL lets each screen request exactly the fields it needs in one round trip, instead of either bloated payloads or a waterfall of REST calls. On a constrained connection this is a real win.
- **Version-locked clients.** Users run old app versions for months — you can't force-upgrade them. (Note this is exactly the problem GraphQL was built at Facebook to solve — for their mobile app.)

**What should decide it for you**

- Choose **GraphQL** if: you control both client and server, you have many varied screens with volatile data requirements, you're iterating product fast, and the team can absorb the extra server-side discipline (caching, query-cost limits, schema governance).
- Choose **REST** if: the API is mostly straightforward CRUD, you lean on CDN/HTTP caching, you have third-party or external consumers, or the team has no GraphQL experience and you want fewer moving parts.

**What would change this recommendation**

The REST default is conditional, and a few specific facts would flip it. Lean toward GraphQL if any of these turn out true: you have more than roughly 15–20 distinct screens whose data shapes diverge and change every sprint; profiling shows real round-trip latency or payload bloat from REST over-/under-fetching on your target networks; or one team owns both ends and is already fluent in GraphQL. Conversely, what would *cement* REST is finding that your caching strategy depends heavily on the CDN/HTTP layer, that you have external or third-party API consumers, or that nobody on the team has run a GraphQL server in production. The main limitation of this answer is that it's a prior, not a measurement — it assumes a fairly conventional app; if your actual screen count, network conditions, or team skills land at an extreme, measure those directly rather than trusting the default.

A practical middle path worth knowing: you can get most of GraphQL's mobile benefit by putting a **backend-for-frontend (BFF)** in front of REST services — purpose-built compound endpoints shaped for each screen. That captures "one round trip, exactly the right data" without taking on a full GraphQL runtime.

If you can tell me how many distinct screens/data shapes you expect, whether one team owns both ends, and how much you rely on caching, I can turn this into a firm single pick rather than a conditional one.
P:
For a typical mobile app, **default to REST.** Move to **GraphQL** only when you have a concrete driver — most commonly: many screens with very different, frequently-changing data needs, owned by one team that controls both the app and the API.

Here's the reasoning, weighted toward what actually bites you on mobile.

**Why REST is the safer default**

- **HTTP caching is free and mature.** REST resources cache cleanly at the CDN, proxy, and client layers through standard cache headers — directly cutting latency and data usage on flaky mobile networks. GraphQL typically POSTs every request to a single endpoint, so you lose HTTP-layer caching and rebuild it client-side with a normalized cache (Apollo, Relay, urql). You can claw some of it back with persisted queries sent over GET, but that's extra setup, not a default.
- **Simpler to operate and secure.** No resolver design, no N+1 query traps, no need for query depth- and cost-limiting to stop a buggy or malicious client from issuing one expensive deeply-nested query. The attack surface is smaller and more familiar.
- **Easier to staff and debug.** More engineers know REST cold, and debugging is usually just a URL and a status code.

**Why mobile, specifically, pushes some teams to GraphQL**

Two real mobile pains GraphQL handles better than REST:

- **Over- and under-fetching.** Each screen wants a slightly different slice of data, and bandwidth costs the user. GraphQL lets a screen request exactly the fields it needs in one round trip — instead of a bloated payload or a waterfall of REST calls. On a constrained connection that's a genuine win.
- **Long-lived old app versions.** Users run old builds for months and you can't force-upgrade them, so the API has to serve many client versions at once. Both REST and GraphQL can evolve additively here — the real difference is visibility. Because every GraphQL client declares exactly which fields it uses, the server knows precisely who depends on what and can deprecate fields with confidence. REST gives you less of that visibility, so teams reach for `/v2`, `/v3` sooner and then maintain them for years. (This is the problem GraphQL was originally built at Facebook to solve — for their mobile app.)

**The decision rule**

- Go **GraphQL** if you own both client and server, have many varied screens with volatile data needs, are iterating product fast, and the team can absorb the extra server-side discipline: caching strategy, query-cost limits, schema governance.
- Stay **REST** if the API is mostly straightforward CRUD, you lean on CDN/HTTP caching, you have third-party or external consumers, or the team has no GraphQL experience and wants fewer moving parts.

**A middle path worth knowing.** You can capture most of GraphQL's mobile benefit by putting a **backend-for-frontend (BFF)** in front of REST services: purpose-built compound endpoints, each shaped for one screen. That buys you "one round trip, exactly the right data" without running a full GraphQL server.

For most teams shipping a new mobile app, that BFF-over-REST path is where I'd start — it's the REST default with the main mobile pain already handled, and you can graduate to GraphQL later if screen variety outgrows it. Tell me your rough screen count, whether one team owns both ends, and how much you rely on caching, and I'll collapse this into a single firm pick.
